--- Page 1 ---
What is work? (M1.1)
Woojin Park
Life Enhancing Technology Laboratory
SNU Department of Industrial Engineering

--- Page 2 ---
What Is Work? Why Definition Matters
• “Work” feels obvious in daily

--- Page 7 ---
Sociological Perspective on Work
• Implication for work system design:
– Technology changes:
• How tasks are performed, and, also
• Who decides
• Who coordinates
• Who is responsible
• How performance is judged

--- Page 8 ---
Psychological Perspective on Work
• Distinction:
– Outcomes (e.g., sales)
– Pe

--- Page 12 ---
Psychological Perspective on Work
• Task Meaning in Job Design
– Meaningfulness arises from
• Task significance (impact on others)
• Task identity (completing a whole task)
• Skill variety
– Definition: Perceived importance and value of the task
– Example
• Low: repetitive fragment with no context
• Hig

--- Page 17 ---
Human Factors / Ergonomics Perspective
• What Is “Unnecessary Load”?
– Load types
• Physical load (force, posture, repetition)
• Cognitive load (memory, attention, decision effort)
• Emotional load (stress, conflict)
– Unnecessary load = strain that does not create value
– Examples:
• Searching for tool

--- Page 24 ---
Information Systems Perspective
• Common mistake:
– Treating “system” as just IT
• Work system thinking:
– Proper unit of analysis = work system
– Includes:
• People
• Processes
• Information
• Technology
• Customers
Alter’s work system framework

--- Page 25 ---
Information Systems Perspective
• Why IT

--- Page 29 ---
User (Worker) Experience Perspective
• User Experience: Work as Lived Interaction
– Work is not only what is done, but how it is experienced
– UX treats work as goal-directed activity situated in a work domain 
(context)
– Users engage in sensory, cognitive, and physical actions
– Work includes employme

--- Page 33 ---
User (Worker) Experience Perspective
• Interaction Design as Coupling Architecture
– Interaction design determines how components connect in practice.
It structures:
• Information access (visibility of system state)
• Task sequencing (workflow logic)
• Control design and input mappings
• Feedback loops

--- Page 36 ---
Mini-Summary
• Work can be understood at multiple levels:
– Behavior
– Task
– Process
– Work system
• Across disciplines, a common conclusion:
– Work is organized activity
– It produces value
– It operates through socio-technical arrangements

behavior

Soldiering and System Misalignment
• Natural soldiering: conserving effort
• Systematic soldiering: deliberate output restriction (Taylor, 
1911)
• Causes:
– Worker mistrust
– Fear of wage cuts
– Lack of objective standards
• Root problem: flawed work organization

The Shovel Studies
• Problem:
Different materials (coal, ore, coke) required different loads.
Workers used identical shovel sizes.
• Taylor’s experiment:
– Determined optimal shovel load ≈ 21 pounds per scoop
– Designed different shovel sizes for different materials
– Standardized tools
– Centralized to

The Ball Bearing Inspection Study
• According to Taylor:
– Workforce reduced: ~120 → ~35 inspectors
– Output increased dramatically
(Taylor reports increase from approx. 5 million to 17 million inspected 
units)
– Accuracy improved (~2/3 improvement in precision)
– Wages increased substantially
– Two pa

Why Taxonomy Matters
• Three complementary taxonomies characterize work systems 
along different dimensions:
– A three-axis structure-based taxonomy
– A two-axis complexity-based taxonomy
– An operation-based work system taxonomy (Professor Jinwoo Park)
• Each taxonomy reveals a different facet of work s

• Low to high
Focus: How cognitively and operationally complex the system is

Complexity-based Taxonomy

text_preview:
--- Page 23 ---
Operation-based Taxonomy
• Simple Work Systems
– Tasks require minimal prior training or expertise
– High repeatability and low variability
– Easily analyzed using:
• Time study
• Motion study
– Human labor is the primary resource
– Examples:
• Manual material handling
• Basic assembly tasks
• Repetitiv

Operation-based Taxonomy
• Project-Based Work Systems
– Produces unique, one-off outputs
– Defined by:
• Clear start and end
• Milestones and deadlines
– High uncertainty and coordination complexity
– Requires:
• Planning tools (e.g., Gantt, PERT/CPM)
– Often involves multidisciplinary teams
– Examples:

--- Page 1 ---
Engineering Problem Solving Process
Woojin Park
Life Enhancing Technology Laboratory
SNU Department of Industrial Engineering

--- Page 2 ---
Engineering Problem Solving Process
• Engin

--- Page 6 ---
Step 1: Define the Problem
• A well-defined problem is already half solved
• Problem definition clarifies what is wrong, for whom, and 
under what conditions
• The key question is not merely “What can we improve?” but 
“What is the actual problem?”
• Poorly defined problems often lead to wasted effort an

--- Page 10 ---
Compare Alternatives and Select the Best One
• Alternatives should be evaluated using explicit criteria
• Typical criteria include:
– Effectiveness
– Cost
– Feasibility
– Safety
– Time
– Compatibility with organizational conditions
• The goal is not to choose the most interesting idea, but the 
most app

--- Page 16 ---
Why Good Ideas Often Fail
• Many good ideas are never adopted
• Technical merit alone does not guarantee implementation
• Resistance to change is one of the biggest barriers to 
improvement
• In practice, an organization may reject useful ideas for social 
or emotional reasons
• Engineering improvement

--- Page 20 ---
Final Takeaways
• Engineering problem solving is systematic rather than 
accidental
• Good improvement begins with clear problem definition and 
careful analysis
• Multiple alternatives should be generated and compared
• Implementation and follow-up are essential parts of the 
process
• Human factors, e

Brainstorming & its variations

Brainstorming?
• Brainstorming is a technique intended to rapidly 
generate a large number of solution ideas to a 
problem
• The method wa

--- Page 5 ---
Basic rules for brainstorming
• Basic rules
– No criticism: think first-judge later
– Focus on quantity (even fragmented ideas or keywords are ok)
• “Quantity bears quality”
– Keep short & snappy
– Don’t settle for the first idea (avoid any fixation)
– Support each other (never criticize)
– Accept unusua

--- Page 12 ---
About the leader
• The leader’s role is to record and write down all the 
ideas
• The leader should not direct or influence the 
brainstorming
• The leader is a facilitator or recorder, not a boss

--- Page 13 ---
Class Activity #1
• Brainstorming exercise

--- Page 14 ---
An “optimization” analogy to b

--- Page 20 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Sequential phases and cognitive efficiency
– Sequential structure:
• Idea purge phase → Idea trigger phase → Evaluation and 
recommendation.
– Benefits:
• Reduces mode switching between mental tasks
– Minimizing mode switching supports the same goal as 
r

--- Page 24 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Production blocking and turn-taking
• Participants must wait to speak:
– Bottlenecks idea flow
– Creates production blocking
• Waiting period:
– Increases risk of forgetting ideas (WM loss)
– Multitasking required: Listen + hold idea in WM

--- Page 25 --

--- Page 28 ---
Weaknesses of brainstorming
• May be limited to relatively simple or well-defined problems
• Generated ideas can be low-quality or superficial without proper 
facilitation
• May be less efficient than individual work for certain tasks (low return on 
investment of time and effort)
• Risk of group dynami

--- Page 35 ---
Comparison of group brainstorming, individual 
brainstorming and brainwriting
Method Advantages Disadvantages
Group 
Brainstorming
- Real-time idea exchange 
and dynamic interaction
- Builds on group energy
- Can spark spontaneous 
ideas
- Production blocking: Since only one person can speak at a time,

--- Page 44 ---
Reverse brainstorming
• Reverse brainstorming helps overcome fixation
• Fixation = Getting stuck on a narrow set of ideas due 
to focused attention
• Reversing the problem changes how it is viewed, 
which changes what is retrieved from long-term 
memory
